entries.intro: Something's been leaving tracks near {subject_focus_the}. Big ones, deep into the mud, and they don't match anything I've seen in thirty years of living here.
details: Three toes, clawed, wider than my hand spread flat. The stride between prints is longer than I am tall.
They circle the same area every night. Whatever it is, it's watching something.
The tracks stop at the tree line and don't come back out. Like it just vanished.
reactions: I don't scare easy, but I stopped walking that path after dark.
People keep saying it's a bear. It's not a bear. Bears don't walk in circles.

statCheck.pass: There were smaller tracks alongside the big ones, just for a stretch. Like something was being led.
statCheck.fail: The mud is too churned up out there. I already told you everything worth seeing.

entries.intro: A shepherd went missing near {subject_focus_the} about a week ago. His flock came back on their own, but he didn't.
details: His crook was found stuck in the ground, straight up, like someone planted it there on purpose.
The sheep were calm when they wandered back. Not spooked at all. That's the part that bothers me.
His dog showed up two days later. Won't stop whimpering and won't go near the eastern ridge.
reactions: His wife keeps a candle lit for him. I don't have the heart to tell her what I think happened.
We sent a search party, but nobody wanted to go past the ridge. Can't say I blame them.

entries.intro: Found three deer near {subject_focus_the}, all dead in a line. No wounds, no blood. Just lying there like they dropped mid-step.
details: Their eyes were open. All three facing the same direction, toward the old stone circle.
No scavengers touched them. Not a single crow, not a fox. Three days and nothing.
The ground under the first deer was warm when I checked. Not sunlight warm: it was before dawn. Something underneath was giving off heat.
reactions: I've hunted these woods my whole life. Animals don't just die like that.
The butcher wouldn't take the meat. Said it smelled wrong, even though it looked fresh.

statCheck.pass: I didn't mention this to anyone else, but the grass under the deer was dead too. A perfect outline, like their shadow burned into the ground.
statCheck.fail: Whatever was there has been rained on and trampled since. I went back myself and there was nothing new to see.

entries.intro: There's a sound coming from the caves near {subject_focus_the} at night. Low and steady, like breathing, but too slow and too deep to be anything alive.
details: It starts just after sundown and fades before first light. Every single night for the past two weeks.
The ground near the cave mouth vibrates when it happens. You can feel it through your boots.
A miner left a tin cup near the entrance overnight. By morning it had slid three feet toward the cave mouth, like something was pulling at the metal.
reactions: The children think it's a sleeping giant. I wish I could believe something that simple.
Two of the miners refuse to go back in until someone explains it. I can't say I disagree with them.

statCheck.pass: I listened carefully one night and realized the rhythm changes: it speeds up when someone gets close to the entrance, then slows again when they back off.
statCheck.fail: Whatever pattern exists is too faint to pick out from this distance. I have listened for hours and gotten nothing more.

entries.intro: The well water near {subject_focus_the} turned bitter overnight. Not just off-tasting: animals won't drink it, and the ones that did got sick.
details: Two goats and a mule drank from the trough before anyone noticed. The goats recovered, but the mule hasn't stood up since.
There's a faint sheen on the surface, oily-looking, and it doesn't mix when you stir it.
The deeper wells are fine for now. But the shallow ones all went bad at the same time.
reactions: We're hauling water from the river until someone figures it out. It's manageable, but it's a lot of extra work.
I keep thinking about what could poison three wells at once. Nothing good comes to mind.

entries.intro: There was a fire near {subject_focus_the} three nights ago. Burned a perfect circle into the hilltop, about twenty paces across. No lightning, no campfire, no explanation.
details: The ash is white, not gray. And the soil underneath is baked hard as pottery.
Everything inside the circle burned completely, but the grass right at the edge wasn't even singed.
There are no scorch trails leading in or out. Whatever caused it started in the center and stayed contained, like it was meant to burn exactly that shape.
reactions: Some people are calling it a sign. I'm calling it a reason to sleep with one eye open.
I've seen wildfires and I've seen lightning strikes. This wasn't either of those.

statCheck.pass: I went back to the circle at dusk and the ground was warm. Not from the sun: the warmth was coming from below, from deep down.
statCheck.fail: The ash has been scattered by wind and foot traffic. Hard to tell much from what is left now.

entries.intro: The wolves near {subject_focus_the} have been acting wrong. Not aggressive: the opposite. They sit at the edge of the village at night and just watch.
details: Six of them, every night, same spot by the tree line. They don't hunt, don't howl. Just sit there.
When you walk toward them, they don't run. They stand up, look at you, then sit back down.
The pack used to stay deep in the hills. Something drove them closer, and whatever it is, they seem more afraid of it than they are of us.
reactions: It's the stillness that gets to me. Wolves are supposed to move. These ones just stare.
Half the village thinks they're protecting us. The other half thinks they're waiting for something. Neither option helps me sleep.

entries.intro: A merchant heading through {subject_focus_the} turned back halfway and warned everyone not to take the eastern road. Wouldn't say why.
details: He was pale and his hands were shaking. This is a man who runs the northern passes in winter: he doesn't rattle easy.
He just said the road was wrong. That's the word he used: wrong. Not blocked, not dangerous. Wrong.
His horse wouldn't go back either. Planted its hooves at the crossroads and refused to face east. He had to blindfold it just to get it turned around.
reactions: Two other traders have since gone around the long way without anyone asking them to. That tells me he wasn't exaggerating.
I pressed him for details over a drink, but he just shook his head and changed the subject.

statCheck.pass: After a few drinks he let one thing slip: he said the road was there, but the landmarks were in the wrong places. Like the land itself had shifted.
statCheck.fail: He will not say more. I pressed him once and he just gave me a long look and said he had already said more than he should have.

entries.intro: The birds around {subject_focus_the} left. All of them, all at once, three mornings ago. Haven't seen a single one since.
details: It wasn't migration: wrong season, wrong direction. They went south in a hurry, every species at once.
The nests are still full. Eggs, chicks, everything abandoned. They didn't plan to leave.
The silence is thick enough to notice. No birdsong, no wing noise, nothing. You hear your own heartbeat when you stand in the clearing now.
reactions: My grandmother used to say when the birds go quiet, you listen. When they leave, you follow.
The silence is the worst part. You don't realize how much noise they made until it's gone.

entries.intro: Someone found claw marks on the watchtower near {subject_focus_the}. High up, about fifteen feet off the ground, gouged deep into the stone.
details: Four parallel grooves, each one as wide as my finger. The stone around them is cracked like it was hit with a chisel.
They're only on the north face. Whatever made them came from one direction and didn't bother going around.
The guard on duty that night says he heard scraping but thought it was the wind. He's not sleeping well.
reactions: Stone doesn't scratch easy. Whatever did that had strength and intent. That combination worries me.
We doubled the night watch. Not because anyone thinks it'll help: because nobody wants to be alone up there.

statCheck.pass: I climbed up to look at the marks myself. There's a residue in the grooves: dark, almost like dried sap, but it smells like iron.
statCheck.fail: From the ground they just look like weathered stone. Someone would need to climb up there to see anything useful.

entries.intro: Part of the old wall near {subject_focus_the} collapsed two days ago. No storm, no tremor. It just came down in the middle of the night.
details: The stones didn't tumble outward like a push. They fell straight down, like the mortar just gave up holding them.
It was the thickest section of the wall. The weakest parts are still standing, which makes no sense.
A runner checked the foundation that morning. Said the base stones were cracked from below, not from any surface impact.
reactions: That wall has stood for longer than anyone here can remember. Walls don't just decide to fall.
We've blocked the gap with carts for now. Nobody's slept well since.

statCheck.pass: I looked at the collapsed stones closely. The cracks on the base aren't random: they follow the same curving line, like something pressed up from underneath.
statCheck.fail: It's a pile of rubble now. I wouldn't know what to look for even if the stones were still in place.

entries.intro: The creek near {subject_focus_the} rose a full two feet overnight. No rain upstream, no snowmelt. The water just came up and hasn't gone back down.
details: It flooded the lower fields and ruined a week's worth of planting. The soil is still waterlogged.
The water itself looks normal: clear, no smell, no color. Just more of it than there should be.
Two families near the bank had to move their stores to higher ground. They're staying with neighbors until it recedes.
reactions: Water does what it wants, I know that. But water doesn't come from nowhere.
I keep checking the bank every morning. It hasn't dropped a single inch in five days.

statCheck.pass: I followed the creek upstream for half a day. There's a new channel feeding into it that wasn't there a month ago. The water is coming from underground, somewhere past the old ruins.
statCheck.fail: I'm no expert on water or land. It rose, and it hasn't gone down. That's all I can tell you.

entries.intro: Three people near {subject_focus_the} came down with the same sickness in the same week. Fever, chills, and a rash that spreads in rings across the skin.
details: The herbalist says she's never seen a rash pattern like it. Concentric rings, almost too even to be natural.
All three of them had been working the same stretch of ground near the old foundation. That's the only thing connecting them.
The fever breaks after a few days, but the rash stays. It fades slowly and leaves a mark like a pale scar.
reactions: People are being careful now. Nobody goes near that stretch of ground without covering their hands and face.
It's probably nothing. Fevers come and go. But the rings on the skin bother me. Sickness shouldn't look so deliberate.

statCheck.pass: You're right to look at their hands. The rash started on the palms and spread outward: they all touched the same thing. I think it's in the soil near those old stones.
statCheck.fail: Sickness is sickness. I'm not a healer and I wouldn't know what symptoms to watch for beyond the obvious.

entries.intro: Someone's been tampering with equipment near {subject_focus_the}. Ropes frayed halfway through, cart axles scored with a blade, straps cut just enough to hold until they're loaded.
details: A cart lost a wheel on the slope last week. The axle pin had been filed down: clean cuts, not wear.
A hauling rope snapped under a load that should've been easy. The fibers were severed partway through, then left to break under strain.
It's always the gear that gets used on the outer routes. Nothing inside the settlement has been touched.
reactions: Whoever is doing this isn't trying to steal. They're trying to hurt someone and make it look like an accident.
People are checking their gear three times before heading out now. That kind of suspicion eats at a community.

statCheck.pass: You've got a keen eye. The cuts on the rope are angled: left-handed, and shallow enough to miss on a quick inspection. That narrows things down.
statCheck.fail: Rope wears, axles crack. I know what I think, but I can't prove the difference between sabotage and bad luck just by looking.

entries.intro: Something killed four goats in a pen near {subject_focus_the} overnight. The fence wasn't broken and the gate was still latched from outside.
details: The wounds are wrong for any predator I know. Single puncture each, deep, right behind the skull. Clean and precise.
There was no struggle. The other animals in the pen weren't panicked come morning: just standing there like nothing happened.
No blood on the ground. Whatever did this either drained them or the blood went somewhere I can't account for.
reactions: A wolf tears. A cat claws. Nothing I've seen kills like a needle and leaves the rest of the herd calm.
The farmer wants to move the rest of the stock inside the walls. I don't blame him, but I'm not sure walls will matter.

statCheck.pass: I checked the pen posts that morning. There's a thin scrape along the top rail, almost invisible: something slid over the fence without climbing it. Smooth and continuous, like a body being dragged across.
statCheck.fail: The pen looks normal from where I stood. I didn't get close enough to examine every board and I'm not sure I'd know what to look for.

entries.intro: There's been smoke rising from somewhere past {subject_focus_the} for three days now. Thin column, steady, but nobody has found the source.
details: Two scouts went out to find it. They walked for hours in the direction it should be and never reached anything burning.
The smoke doesn't behave like a campfire. It rises perfectly straight even when the wind is blowing. No drift at all.
It's only visible from certain angles. Walk a quarter mile east and you can't see it anymore. Come back and there it is.
reactions: Smoke means fire, and fire means someone or something. The fact that we can't find either one is the problem.
I've stopped pointing it out to people. Half of them can't see it from where they stand and they look at me like I'm making it up.

statCheck.pass: I watched it carefully at sunset. The smoke doesn't cast a shadow. Everything else out there does, but the column has no shadow at all.
statCheck.fail: From this distance it could be anything: a cookfire, a brush pile, heat shimmer. I can't make out more than the shape.

entries.intro: The insects near {subject_focus_the} have been swarming in ways I've never seen. Thick clouds of them gathering over the same patch of ground every evening.
details: They're not biting, not feeding, not mating. Just circling in a column about ten feet across, rising and falling together.
Every species mixed in together: flies, beetles, moths, things that don't normally swarm at all. They just hover there until dawn.
The patch of ground underneath is bare. Whatever was growing there died, and the insects arrived the same week.
reactions: Insects don't bother me as a rule. But watching a thousand of them move as one body puts a chill in you.
The children wanted to run through the swarm. We told them no. Can't explain why exactly, but every part of me said no.

statCheck.pass: I noticed something else: the swarm is denser at the center, right over a spot where the ground is slightly sunken. Like something underneath is drawing them.
statCheck.fail: Bugs swarm. They just do. I don't have the knowledge to tell you why these ones picked that spot.

entries.intro: A stretch of road near {subject_focus_the} has become dangerous. Three carts have broken wheels on the same section in the last ten days.
details: The road surface looks fine from a distance, but up close there are hairline cracks running across the stone. Perfectly straight, evenly spaced.
The cracks weren't there a month ago. A carter who uses that road twice a week swears the stone was solid.
Each break happened in daylight with light loads. These weren't overloaded wagons: they were routine trips.
reactions: We've rerouted the heavy carts through the lower path. It adds half a day but at least nobody's losing wheels.
Roads don't crack from the inside. Something underneath is shifting, and that bothers me more than the broken carts.

statCheck.pass: I knelt by one of the cracks and put my ear to the stone. There's a faint vibration: irregular, like tapping. It's coming from deep below the road bed.
statCheck.fail: Stone cracks for all kinds of reasons: frost, settling, age. I can't tell you which one this is just by looking.

entries.intro: There's been a dead silence in the woods near {subject_focus_the}. No wind in the leaves, no insects, no animal calls. Just nothing.
details: It's not the whole forest: just a stretch about a hundred paces wide. Step inside it and every sound drops away.
You can still hear your own footsteps, your own breathing. But the world around you goes completely quiet.
A woodcutter tried shouting from inside the silent stretch. He said his voice felt muffled, like yelling into wool.
reactions: I walked through it once. Once was enough. The quiet makes you feel like you're being watched by something that doesn't need to breathe.
People are taking the long way around now. Nobody ordered it: they just started doing it.

statCheck.pass: I stood at the edge and watched carefully. The leaves inside the silent stretch are still moving: the wind is there, it just makes no sound. Something is swallowing the noise itself.
statCheck.fail: Quiet woods aren't unusual after a frost or a predator passing through. Could be a dozen explanations that have nothing to do with anything strange.

entries.intro: An old camp was found near {subject_focus_the}. Not recent: the firepit was cold and the bedrolls were stiff with dew. But the way it was left doesn't sit right.
details: Everything was still laid out: cook pot on the stones, packs leaning against a log, boots set by the bedroll. Like someone planned to come right back.
There were meals left in the pot. Uneaten, spoiled now. Whatever made them leave, they didn't stop to pack.
The tracks leaving the camp go in one direction and stop abruptly after about fifty paces. Just end, mid-stride.
reactions: People abandon camps when something scares them. But scared people run: they don't vanish mid-step.
I told the watch captain about it. He marked it on his patrol map but he hasn't sent anyone to investigate.

statCheck.pass: You picked up on it too? The tracks don't just stop: the last footprint is deeper on the toe, like they were lifted. Pulled upward, not forward.
statCheck.fail: Old camps are common enough out there. Without knowing who set it, there's no way to know why they left.

entries.intro: The ground near {subject_focus_the} has been sinking. Not fast: a few inches a week. But the area keeps spreading and nobody knows what's underneath.
details: It started as a shallow dip the size of a market stall. Now it's wider than a house and the center is a full foot lower than it was.
The soil at the edges cracks when you step on it. Dry and hollow-sounding, like there's nothing underneath holding it up.
A fence post near the edge tilted overnight. The bottom of it wasn't resting on anything: just hanging in empty space.
reactions: I keep thinking about what happens when it stops sinking slowly and starts sinking all at once.
The council roped it off. That's their answer to everything: put a rope around it and hope it stops.

statCheck.pass: I dropped a stone into the gap under the fence post. It took a long time to hit bottom. Whatever cavity is under there, it's deep: deeper than any root cellar or mine I know of.
statCheck.fail: Ground settles for all sorts of reasons. Could be an old burrow collapsing, could be drainage. I wouldn't know where to start looking.

entries.intro: A spring near {subject_focus_the} dried up without warning. It's fed this area for as long as anyone can remember, and one morning it was just gone.
details: The basin is still damp but no water flows. The stones at the mouth are dry and cracking from the heat.
Three other springs in the area are fine. It's only this one, which makes no sense: they all draw from the same aquifer.
An old farmer says the water didn't slow down first. One day full, next day nothing. Like someone turned it off.
reactions: We can manage without it for now. But if the others go the same way, we've got a real problem.
Springs don't just stop. Something changed underground, and whatever it is hasn't shown itself yet.

statCheck.pass: I went to the spring mouth at night and put my hand on the rock. It was warm. Not sun-warm: the stone was radiating heat from inside, steady and even.
statCheck.fail: Water does strange things underground. Shifts, finds new channels. I don't have the knowledge to say why this one stopped.

entries.intro: Territorial markings started appearing near {subject_focus_the}. Scratches on stones, broken branches bent at odd angles, piles of rocks stacked in threes.
details: The scratches are deliberate: three vertical lines, always grouped, always on the side of the stone facing the settlement.
The branch bends aren't natural breaks. The wood is twisted, still green, bent with force and held in place with something like dried sinew.
They form a rough line along the ridge. Whatever left them is marking a boundary, and we're on the wrong side of it.
reactions: Animals mark territory. People mark territory. Both of those I can deal with. This doesn't look like either.
The hunters say they've never seen markings like these, and they know every sign the local wildlife leaves.

statCheck.pass: I compared markings from two different sites. The scratches get deeper and wider the closer they are to the settlement. Whatever is making them is getting bolder.
statCheck.fail: Scratches on rocks could be anything: animals sharpening claws, weather cracking the surface. I can't tell the difference without more time.

entries.intro: People near {subject_focus_the} have been hearing things at night. Not animals: a low rhythmic knocking, like someone striking wood with a mallet, coming from the direction of the old ruins.
details: It goes on for about an hour after midnight, always the same tempo. Steady, unhurried, deliberate.
Two guards walked toward it last week. They say it got quieter the closer they got, like it was pulling away from them.
The knocking stopped completely on the night of the new moon. Started again the next night, same rhythm, same direction.
reactions: Knowing when it stops is almost worse than the sound itself. It means whatever is doing it is paying attention to something.
I've heard it from my window. You lie there and count the beats and try to convince yourself it's a branch hitting a wall. It isn't.

statCheck.pass: I counted the intervals between knocks over three nights. The spacing is getting shorter: not by much, but it's measurable. Whatever the pattern is, it's accelerating.
statCheck.fail: Sound carries strangely at night, especially near old stone. I've heard plenty of odd noises and most of them turned out to be nothing.

entries.intro: A patch of plants appeared near {subject_focus_the} that nobody can identify. Dark leaves, almost black, growing in a tight cluster where nothing grew before.
details: They came up fast: bare ground three days ago, knee-high plants now. Nothing grows that quickly, not even weeds.
The leaves have a sharp smell when you get close. Not rot, not flowers: something metallic, like heated iron.
A ring of dead grass surrounds the patch, about two feet wide. Everything that was growing near them withered and dried up.
reactions: The herbalist won't touch them. She said if something grows that fast and kills everything around it, you leave it alone.
I've been farming this land for twenty years. I know every weed and wildflower within a day's walk. These aren't from here.

statCheck.pass: You're right to keep your distance. I broke a leaf off with a stick and the sap was dark red: thick, almost like blood. The stick started to discolor where the sap touched it.
statCheck.fail: Plants grow where they want to grow. I'm not the person to ask about what species it is or where it came from.

entries.intro: The foundations of the old storehouse near {subject_focus_the} have started cracking. Not settling cracks: deep splits running through stone that's been solid for generations.
details: The cracks run in straight lines, corner to corner, like the building was pulled apart from two directions at once.
The upper walls are fine. It's only the foundation and the first few courses of stone. Whatever force did this came from below.
Dust keeps falling from the ceiling inside. Slow and constant, like the whole structure is quietly vibrating.
reactions: We moved the grain stores out as a precaution. If that building comes down, I'd rather lose the building than the food.
Foundations don't crack on their own. Something is wrong with the ground underneath, and the storehouse is just the first thing showing it.

statCheck.pass: I pressed my palm flat on the foundation wall. There's a pulse: faint, rhythmic, nothing like settling or wind. Something underneath that building is moving.
statCheck.fail: Old buildings crack. It's what they do. Without a mason's eye, I can't say whether these cracks are normal or not.

entries.intro: {npc_name} told me something got into the food cellar near {subject_focus_the}. Didn't take anything: just ruined it. Every sack of grain split open, every jar cracked, every barrel tipped.
details: The cellar door was still barred from the outside. However it got in, it didn't use the door.
The damage was thorough. Not random thrashing: every single container was opened or broken. Methodical.
There was a smell down there afterward. Not the spoiled food: something else. Sharp and sour, like vinegar mixed with copper.
reactions: Rats get into cellars. Thieves get into cellars. Neither of them bars the door behind them and ruins everything without taking a bite.
We can replace the food. What I can't replace is the feeling of knowing something got inside without anyone seeing how.

statCheck.pass: You've got a good nose for what matters. The smell was strongest near the back wall, where the stone meets the earth. There's a hairline gap there now that wasn't there before.
statCheck.fail: The cellar's been cleaned out and restocked already. Whatever was down there left no sign I could recognize.

entries.intro: A hunter near {subject_focus_the} came back from the ridge with burns on his arms. Says he didn't touch fire, didn't cook, didn't even carry a torch.
details: The burns are on both forearms, symmetrical, like he reached through something. Blistered and raw, the kind you'd get from pressing against hot metal.
He says he was walking a game trail and felt a sudden wave of heat. Lasted maybe two heartbeats, then it was gone.
The healer dressed the burns and said the skin damage goes deeper than a surface burn should. Whatever the heat was, it wasn't ordinary flame.
reactions: He's not the kind to make things up. Quiet man, practical. If he says it happened, it happened.
Nobody wants to use that trail now. I don't blame them, but it's the fastest route to the upper pastures.

statCheck.pass: He mentioned one more thing he didn't tell the others: there was a smell right before the heat. Like hot stone and rust. And the air shimmered, even though it was overcast.
statCheck.fail: Burns are burns. I don't know enough about what causes them to say anything useful beyond what the healer already told us.

entries.intro: The fence line around the outer pasture near {subject_focus_the} keeps getting torn apart at night. Not pushed over: the posts are pulled clean out of the ground.
details: The posts aren't broken. They're removed whole, dropped a few paces away, like they were lifted and set down.
It happens in the same section every time. We repair it during the day and it's torn out again by morning.
The holes where the posts were pulled from are clean. No digging, no churning. The post just comes straight up out of packed earth.
reactions: We've driven the posts deeper and packed stones around the base. It doesn't help. They still come out clean.
It feels like a message. Whatever is doing this doesn't want a fence there, and it's making that very clear.

statCheck.pass: I stayed up one night to watch. Never saw anything. But in the morning, the posts were out again: and my boot prints from the night showed I'd been standing twenty paces closer to the fence than I remembered. I must have fallen asleep, but I don't remember sleeping.
statCheck.fail: Could be livestock rubbing against the posts, could be the soil being too loose. I've seen both cause fence trouble before.

entries.intro: Two families near {subject_focus_the} have reported the same thing: something on their rooftops at night. Heavy enough to creak the beams, but gone before anyone gets outside to look.
details: It's not footsteps: more of a slow drag, like something heavy sliding across the thatch from one end to the other.
Both houses are on the edge of the settlement, closest to the open ground. The inner buildings haven't reported anything.
One family found scratches on their chimney stones in the morning. Shallow gouges, as though something gripped the stonework to hold itself in place.
reactions: Heavy things on rooftops that leave scratches aren't wind or cats. I wish people would stop suggesting otherwise.
The families asked to move in with relatives closer to the center. Nobody argued.

statCheck.pass: I checked both rooftops myself. The thatch isn't just scraped: there are impressions pressed into it. Long, narrow, too evenly spaced to be random. Something with reach was lying flat against the roof.
statCheck.fail: Rooftops make all kinds of noises, especially old ones. Beams settle, animals nest. Without seeing it happen, I wouldn't know what to blame.
